Day15 - Learning ThinkPHP from Scratch: Model Basics (3)
Last time we learned how to insert data through the Model. But what about reading it back? How do you actually query data? So in this post I'll walk through how to use the Model to do queries.
First we need to register two routes, otherwise nothing below will work:
language-php shiki shiki-themes github-light github-light github-dark-high-contrast

Route::get('/about/list', 'about/index/all');
Route::get('/about/:id', 'about/index/select'); // This is a dynamic route. I'll explain it more later.
// This is a dynamic route. I'll explain it more later.

We have a way to query a single record by id, so naturally there's also a way to query everything. For Model operations, we'll use the
. But the data that comes back has to be handled with a loop, because what you get back is an array. So let's look at the example code~
Basically this is enough to pull all the data and return it to the homepage ↓
But here I'll write a second example. Let's try sending the data to the frontend, so we'll use
to render it onto a specific HTML template:
// Don't forget to return on the last line, otherwise you won't see the template~
Next we need to create a getAllUser.html under
. I'll provide the contents directly — here's the example:
language-html shiki shiki-themes github-light github-light github-dark-high-contrast

{$title}

{volist name="user" id="list"}
  • 姓名:{$list.name}
  • 體重:{$list.weight}
  • 身高:{$list.height}
{/volist}
Don't worry if you see something unfamiliar in there — I'll explain it in a moment. Once you've created it, you can visit
to check whether the data loads successfully ↓
Data list rendered on the frontend
And just like that, we've successfully fetched all the user data and rendered it on the frontend.
Finally, let me circle back to that weird little bit inside the HTML, namely
is a built-in tag that ThinkPHP provides, and it can loop over the data we passed in from the backend. I'll cover these built-in tags one by one later on, but in practice the ones you'll use most are
for now — we'll have a chance to use it later — and I'll give these built-in tags a more detailed write-up in a later chapter.
In the next chapter I'm going to step away from the

series for a bit and jump over to raw data operations. After all, sometimes we may not want to go through the Model to work with data — we just want to do it inside the Controller. So the next post will hop over to data operations to get a feel for the difference between manipulating things through the Model versus writing your own SQL. Last time we learned how to insert data through the Model. But what about reading it back? How do you actually query data? So this post is all about using the Model to pull records — fetching a single row by id, grabbing the whole list, and even passing that data to the frontend to render it.
